content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 3 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 198–200 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_003_page_001.jpg, DEU_003_page_002.jpg, DEU_003_page_003.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 145–146

---

1 And we turned and went up the way of the Bashan, and Og king of the Bashan came out to meet us — he and all his people — for battle at Edre'i.

2 And the LORD said to me: "Do not fear him, for into your hand I have given him and all his people and his land, and you shall do to him as you did to Sichon king of the Emori who dwelt in Heshbon."

3 And the LORD our God gave into our hand also Og king of the Bashan and all his people, and we struck him until there was left to him no survivor.

4 And we captured all his cities at that time — there was not a town that we did not take from them: sixty cities, all the region of Argov, the kingdom of Og in the Bashan.

5 All these [were] cities fortified with high walls, gates and bars — besides very many unwalled towns.

6 And we devoted them to destruction as we did to Sichon king of Heshbon — devoting to destruction every city: men, women, and little ones.

7 And all the livestock and the spoil of the cities we plundered for ourselves.

8 And we took at that time from the hand of the two kings of the Emori the land that [is] beyond the Yarden — from the Wadi Arnon to Mount Chermon —

9 the Tsidoni'im call Chermon "Siryon," and the Emori call it "Senir" —

10 all the cities of the plateau and all of Gil'ad and all of the Bashan as far as Salkhah and Edre'i, cities of the kingdom of Og in the Bashan.

11 For only Og king of the Bashan remained of the remnant of the Refa'im. Behold, his bedstead [was] a bedstead of iron — is it not in Rabbah of the sons of Ammon? Nine cubits [is] its length and four cubits its width, by the cubit of a man.

12 And this land — we took possession of it at that time: from Aro'er which is by the Wadi Arnon and half the hill-country of Gil'ad and its cities I gave to the Re'uveni and to the Gadi.

13 And the rest of Gil'ad and all the Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to the half-tribe of Menashe — all the region of Argov; all that Bashan is called "the land of the Refa'im."

14 Ya'ir son of Menashe took all the region of Argov as far as the border of the Geshurite and the Ma'akhati, and he called them — the Bashan — by his own name: Chavvot Ya'ir, to this day.

15 And to Makhir I gave Gil'ad.

16 And to the Re'uveni and to the Gadi I gave from Gil'ad and as far as the Wadi Arnon — the midst of the wadi and the border — and as far as the Wadi Yabbok, which is the border of the sons of Ammon;

17 and the Arabah and the Yarden and the border, from Kinneret and as far as the sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, under the slopes of Pisgah, toward the east.

18 And I commanded you at that time, saying: "The LORD your God has given you this land to possess it. You shall cross over armed before your brothers the sons of Yisra'el, all the able men.

19 Only your wives and your little ones and your livestock — I know that you have much livestock — shall dwell in your cities that I have given to you,

20 until the LORD gives rest to your brothers as [he gave] to you, and they also possess the land that the LORD your God gives to them beyond the Yarden; then each man shall return to his possession that I have given to you."

21 And Yehoshua I commanded at that time, saying: "Your eyes have seen all that the LORD your God has done to these two kings; so shall the LORD do to all the kingdoms into which you are crossing over.

22 You shall not fear them, for the LORD your God — he is fighting for you."

23 And I entreated the LORD at that time, saying:

24 "O Lord GOD, you have begun to show your servant your greatness and your strong hand — for what god [is there] in the heavens or on the earth that can do according to your works and according to your mighty acts?

25 Let me cross over, please, and see the good land that is beyond the Yarden, this good hill-country and the Levanon."

26 But the LORD was angry with me on your account and would not listen to me; and the LORD said to me: "Enough for you — do not add to speak to me further of this matter.

27 Go up to the top of the Pisgah and lift up your eyes westward and northward and southward and eastward, and look with your eyes, for you shall not cross this Yarden.

28 But command Yehoshua and strengthen him and encourage him, for he shall cross before this people and he shall cause them to inherit the land that you shall see."

29 And we dwelt in the valley opposite Beit-Pe'or.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — the way of the Bashan:** *derekh ha-Bashan* — literally "the road of the Bashan," the route northward through the Transjordanian plateau. Bashan (modern Hauran/Golan region) was known for its fertile pastures and oak forests.

**v.4 — the region of Argov:** *chevel Argov* — *chevel* means "cord, line, portion, region." Argov is a specific district within Bashan, location debated — possibly modern Leja. The LXX (p. 145): *πᾶσαν τὴν χώραν τοῦ Αργοβ* — straightforward.

**v.5 — fortified with high walls, gates and bars:** *betsurot chomah gedolah delatayim u-veriach* — literally "fortified [with] a great wall, double-doors and a bar." The idiom *delatayim* (dual form of *delet*) refers to two-panel city gates. *U-veriach* is the cross-bar that locked them. EMET renders "gates and bars" for the compound.

**v.9 — Siryon / Senir:** Two different non-Israelite names for Chermon (Hermon). This is a rare explicit bilingual gloss in the MT itself — the Phoenicians (Tsidoni'im) called it Siryon; Psalm 29:6 confirms this. The Emorites called it Senir. The LXX (p. 145–146) transliterates: *Σανιρ* for Senir, *Σιριον* for Siryon.

**v.11 — his bedstead [was] a bedstead of iron:** *arso arset barzel* — the intensive construction with *arso* (his bedstead) restated as *arset barzel* (a bedstead of iron). The display of Og's enormous iron bed in Rabbah of Ammon serves as physical proof — presumably still visible at the time of writing — of the giants' historical reality. Nine cubits × four cubits = approximately 4.1 m × 1.8 m in standard cubit, larger if a royal cubit. The LXX: *κλίνη σιδηρᾶ* — iron bed; it does not repeat *arso* but the dimensions agree.

**v.12 — from Aro'er... half the hill-country of Gil'ad:** The tribal allotments across Transjordan. EMET notes the precision: *chatsi har ha-Gil'ad* (half the hill-country of Gilead) went to Re'uven and Gad; the other half plus all Bashan to half-Menashe (v.13).

**v.14 — Chavvot Ya'ir:** *Chavvot Ya'ir* — "the tent-villages of Ya'ir." *Chavvot* (from *chavah* or *chayah*) refers to pastoral encampments or unwalled settlements. The LXX: *Αὐχωθ Ιαΐρ* — a transliteration. The phrase "to this day" (*ad ha-yom ha-zeh*) is a Deuteronomic formula indicating the author's present, which in the fiction of the narrative is shortly before Moses' death. The actual settlement by Ya'ir is narrated in Numbers 32:41.

**v.17 — slopes of Pisgah:** *ashdot ha-pisgah* — literally "the poured-out places [water-runoffs] of Pisgah" — the slopes descending from the Pisgah ridge toward the Jordan Valley. The LXX: *ὑπὸ τὴν Φασγα* (under the Fasga). The Salt Sea = the Dead Sea; Kinneret = the Sea of Galilee.

**v.23 — I entreated the LORD:** *va-etchannan el-YHWH* — the Hithpa'el of *chanan* (to be gracious), meaning to implore grace, to plead for favor. This gives the rabbinic name for the Torah portion containing this chapter: *Va-etchannan*. The prayer is one of the most personal moments in Moshe's speeches.

**v.24 — O Lord GOD:** *Adonai YHWH* — the double divine name. The first (*Adonai*) is not YHWH but the title "Lord/Master"; together they form the compound used in solemn address. The LXX: *Κύριε Κύριε* (Lord Lord) — the doubling reflects the two titles.

**v.25 — this good hill-country and the Levanon:** *ha-har ha-tov ha-zeh ve-ha-Levanon* — the "good mountain" may refer to the Jerusalem hill-country specifically, or more broadly to the western highlands. The Levanon's inclusion in the desired view may be a poetic reach northward. The LXX (p. 146): *τὸ ὄρος τοῦτο τὸ ἀγαθὸν καὶ τὸν Ἀντιλίβανον* — "this good mountain and the Anti-Lebanon." Notably, Vaticanus reads *Ἀντιλίβανον* (Anti-Lebanon) rather than *Λίβανον* — a variant absent from MT.

**v.26 — Enough for you:** *rav lakh* — literally "great/much for you." An idiom of cessation, implying "you have had enough of asking." The same idiom is used for Korach and his company (Numbers 16:3) and for Eliyahu (1 Kings 19:4). The LORD closes the prayer. The LXX: *ἱκανούσθω σοι* — "let it suffice for you."

**v.27 — Pisgah:** The peak from which Moshe views the land. Deuteronomy 34 will narrate his death and burial there. The four directions named — *yammah ve-tsaphonah ve-temanah u-mizrachah* — constitute a full compass survey of the promised land.

**v.28 — strengthen him and encourage him:** *ve-chazzeqhu ve-amtsehu* — two Pi'el imperatives, both meaning to make strong but with slightly different nuances: *chazaq* emphasizes strength of grip/resolution; *amets* emphasizes bold courage. The LXX: *κατίσχυσον αὐτὸν καὶ παρακάλεσον αὐτόν* — "strengthen him and encourage him." *Parakalesón* (encourage, console) renders *amets* somewhat differently, suggesting moral support.

**v.29 — Beit-Pe'or:** The location associated with the Baal-Pe'or apostasy of Numbers 25. The name is recalled again at 4:3. The valley opposite it was the campsite for Moshe's final addresses.

— EMET
